title: On The Receipt Of My Motherï؟½s Picture Out Of Norfolk, The Gift Of My Cousin, Ann Bodham.
content:
آ آ آ آ O that those lips had language! Life has passï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With me but roughly since I heard thee last.
آ آ آ آ Those lips are thineï؟½thy own sweet smile I see,
آ آ آ آ The same that oft in childhood solaced me;
آ آ آ آ Voice only fails, else how distinct they say,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Grieve not, my child, chase all thy fears away!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ The meek intelligence of those dear eyes
آ آ آ آ (Blest be the art that can immortalize,
آ آ آ آ The art that baffles Timeï؟½s tyrannic claim
آ آ آ آ To quench it) here shines on me still the same.
آ آ آ آ Faithful remembrancer of one so dear,
آ آ آ آ O welcome guest, though unexpected here:
آ آ آ آ Who bidst me honour with an artless song,
آ آ آ آ Affectionate, a mother lost so long.
آ آ آ آ I will obey, not willingly alone,
آ آ آ آ But gladly, as the precept were her own:
آ آ آ آ And, while that face renews my filial grief,
آ آ آ آ Fancy shall weave a charm for my relief,
آ آ آ آ Shall steep me in Elysian reverie,
آ آ آ آ A momentary dream, that thou art she.
آ آ آ آ My mother! when I learnï؟½d that thou wast dead,
آ آ آ آ Say, wast thou conscious of the tears I shed?
آ آ آ آ Hoverï؟½d thy spirit oï؟½er thy sorrowing son,
آ آ آ آ Wretch even then, lifeï؟½s journey just begun?
آ آ آ آ Perhaps thou gavest me, though unfelt, a kiss;
آ آ آ آ Perhaps a tear, if souls can weep in blissï؟½
آ آ آ آ Ah, that maternal smile! it answersï؟½Yes.
آ آ آ آ I heard the bell tollï؟½d on thy burial day,
آ آ آ آ I saw the hearse that bore thee slow away,
آ آ آ آ And turning from my nursery window, drew
آ آ آ آ A long, long sigh, and wept a last adieu!
آ آ آ آ But was it such?ï؟½It was.ï؟½Where thou art gone,
آ آ آ آ Adieus and farewells are a sound unknown.
آ آ آ آ May I but meet thee on that peaceful shore,
آ آ آ آ The parting word shall pass my lips no more!
آ آ آ آ Thy maidens, grieved themselves at my concern,
آ آ آ آ Oft gave me promise of thy quick return.
آ آ آ آ What ardently I wishï؟½d, I long believed,
آ آ آ آ And, disappointed still, was still deceived.
آ آ آ آ By expectation every day beguiled,
آ آ آ آ Dupe of to-morrow even from a child.
آ آ آ آ Thus many a sad to-morrow came and went,
آ آ آ آ Till, all my stock of infant sorrows spent,
آ آ آ آ I learnï؟½d at last submission to my lot,
آ آ آ آ But, though I less deplored thee, neï؟½er forgot.
آ آ آ آ Where once we dwelt our name is heard no more,
آ آ آ آ Children not thine have trod my nursery floor;
آ آ آ آ And where the gardener Robin, day by day,
آ آ آ آ Drew me to school along the public way,
آ آ آ آ Delighted with my bauble coach, and wrappï؟½d
آ آ آ آ In scarlet mantle warm, and velvet cappï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis now become a history little known,
آ آ آ آ That once we callï؟½d the pastoral house our own.
آ آ آ آ Short-lived possession! but the record fair,
آ آ آ آ That memory keeps of all thy kindness there,
آ آ آ آ Still outlives many a storm, that has effaced
آ آ آ آ A thousand other themes less deeply traced.
آ آ آ آ Thy nightly visits to my chamber made,
آ آ آ آ That thou mightst know me safe and warmly laid;
آ آ آ آ Thy morning bounties ere I left my home,
آ آ آ آ The biscuit or confectionary plum;
آ آ آ آ The fragrant waters on my cheeks bestowï؟½d
آ آ آ آ By thy own hand, till fresh they shone and glowï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ All this, and more endearing still than all,
آ آ آ آ Thy constant flow of love that knew no fall,
آ آ آ آ Neï؟½er roughenï؟½d by those cataracts and breaks
آ آ آ آ That humour interposed too often makes;
آ آ آ آ All this still legible in memoryï؟½s page,
آ آ آ آ And still to be so to my latest age,
آ آ آ آ Adds joy to duty, makes me glad to pay
آ آ آ آ Such honours to thee as my numbers may;
آ آ آ آ Perhaps a frail memorial, but sincere,
آ آ آ آ Not scornï؟½d in heaven, though little noticed here.
آ آ آ آ Could Time, his flight reversed, restore the hours,
آ آ آ آ When, playing with thy vestureï؟½s tissued flowers,
آ آ آ آ The violet, the pink, and jessamine,
آ آ آ آ I prickï؟½d them into paper with a pin
آ آ آ آ (And thou wast happier than myself the while,
آ آ آ آ Wouldst softly speak, and stroke my head, and smile),
آ آ آ آ Could those few pleasant days again appear,
آ آ آ آ Might one wish bring them, would I wish them here?
آ آ آ آ I would not trust my heartï؟½the dear delight
آ آ آ آ Seems so to be desired, perhaps I might.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ But noï؟½what here we call our life is such,
آ آ آ آ So little to be loved, and thou so much,
آ آ آ آ That I should ill requite thee to constrain
آ آ آ آ Thy unbound spirit into bonds again.
آ آ آ آ Thou, as a gallant bark from Albionï؟½s coast
آ آ آ آ (The storms all weatherï؟½d and the ocean crossï؟½d),
آ آ آ آ Shoots into port at some well-havenï؟½d isle
آ آ آ آ Where spices breathe, and brighter seasons smile,
آ آ آ آ There sits quiescent on the floods, that show
آ آ آ آ Her beauteous form reflected clear below,
آ آ آ آ While airs impregnated with incense play
آ آ آ آ Around her, fanning light her streamers gay;
آ آ آ آ So thou, with sails how swift! hast reachï؟½d the shore,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Where tempests never beat nor billows roar;ï؟½[1]
آ آ آ آ And thy loved consort on the dangerous tide
آ آ آ آ Of life long since has anchorï؟½d by thy side.
آ آ آ آ But me, scarce hoping to attain that rest,
آ آ آ آ Always from port withheld, always distressï؟½dï؟½
آ آ آ آ Me howling blasts drive devious, tempest-tossï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Sails rippï؟½d, seams opening wide, and compass lost,
آ آ آ آ And day by day some currentï؟½s thwarting force
آ آ آ آ Sets me more distant from a prosperous course.
آ آ آ آ But oh, the thought, that thou art safe, and he!
آ آ آ آ That thought is joy, arrive what may to me.
آ آ آ آ My boast is not that I deduce my birth
آ آ آ آ From loins enthroned, and rulers of the earth;
آ آ آ آ But higher far my proud pretensions riseï؟½
آ آ آ آ The son of parents passï؟½d into the skies.
آ آ آ آ And now, farewellï؟½Time unrevoked has run
آ آ آ آ His wonted course, yet what I wishï؟½d is done.
آ آ آ آ By contemplationï؟½s help, not sought in vain,
آ آ آ آ I seem to have lived my childhood oï؟½er again;
آ آ آ آ To have renewï؟½d the joys that once were mine,
آ آ آ آ Without the sin of violating thine;
آ آ آ آ And, while the wings of fancy still are free,
آ آ آ آ And I can view this mimic show of thee,
آ آ آ آ Time has but half succeeded in his theftï؟½
آ آ آ آ Thyself removed, thy power to soothe me left.
meaning:
"On The Receipt Of My Motherï؟½s Picture Out Of Norfolk, The Gift Of My Cousin, Ann Bodham." is a quintessential example of William Cowper's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
